title: Not a drop to spare
data_sources: City of Cape Town official communications

data_scope.cited_global_study: Florke et al. 2018: over 25% of major world cities could exceed current water resources under climate change
data_scope.per_capita_benchmarks: South Africa 235 L/day; USA >300 L/day; Melbourne 160 L/day

core_conclusion: Cape Town's 2018 Day Zero crisis arose from the convergence of unprecedented drought, reservoir management failures, and slow institutional response — not precipitation alone. Every major world city must prepare comparable emergency plans, because technological solutions, institutional organization, and social behaviour must work in concert. Water crises will recur and intensify globally as climate change and urbanization tighten the demand-supply gap.
why_it_matters: Turns an abstract hydrological risk into a lived, newsworthy case that made urban water insecurity credible to policymakers worldwide; frames the problem as multi-dimensional (hydrology + governance + behaviour); highlights inequality in how conservation burdens are distributed; calls explicitly for multi-disciplinary research on the intersections of engineering, social, and hydrological dimensions.
why_nature: Timely editorial matching Cape Town's global media moment in early 2018; directly anchors Nature Sustainability's launch mission (water/cities/climate/equity); synthesises hydrology, social science, infrastructure, and governance into a policy call; universal relevance across water-stressed cities worldwide.
added_value_to_my_research: Global Day-Zero risk index -> spatiotemporal GeoAI combining ERA5/GRACE-FO/SSP projections as health covariate layer
Spatially heterogeneous nudge effects -> MGWR/GWR analysis of conservation billing microdata by income stratum
Water access-point equity -> R2SFCA applied to emergency water distribution under mobility-stratified demand
Natural + engineered infrastructure fusion -> multi-source remote sensing and hydrological early-warning GeoAI model
Governance latency ABM -> agent-based model coupling reservoir mass-balance with institutional response delay and consumer heterogeneity

related.title: Future global urban water scarcity and potential solutions
related.relation: quantifies global urban water deficit under climate scenarios, operationalizing the editorial's global risk warning

related.title: Water conservation through plumbing and nudging
related.relation: provides causal evidence on behavioural and infrastructure interventions for urban water conservation — directly extends the editorial's social behaviour dimension

related.title: Urban water crises driven by elites' unsustainable consumption
related.relation: empirically tests the editorial's equity conjecture: elite overconsumption as a structural driver of urban water crises

related.title: Natural infrastructure in sustaining global urban freshwater ecosystem services
related.relation: extends the editorial's infrastructure discussion to natural solutions for freshwater ecosystem services

related.title: Water competition between cities and agriculture driven by climate change and urban growth
related.relation: the Florke et al. paper cited within the editorial; quantifies the twin challenge of urban demand growth and climate change

figures.caption: Water conservation mural in Cape Town International Airport passport control hall, December 2017 (photographer: Ryan Scarrow)
